summary: Large asm/data.s cleanup that finished converting many hard-coded data pointer literals, mostly 0x803Fxxxx addresses in pointer tables, into symbolic lbl_803Fxxxx references and added missing global labels at the referenced data blocks. The PR had no body or review discussion, so the rationale is inferred from the title and diff: make pointer-bearing data symbolic, searchable, and easier for future decompilation work to cross-reference.

key_files.role: Only changed file; replaced many raw .4byte address constants with labels and inserted .global lbl_803Fxxxx label definitions for data targets.

what_changed: Changed many `.4byte 0x803Fxxxx` entries in `asm/data.s` pointer tables to `.4byte lbl_803Fxxxx`, for example `0x803F8320` to `lbl_803F8320`, `0x803F8EB0` to `lbl_803F8EB0`, and `0x803F6788` to `lbl_803F6788`.

Added missing `.global lbl_803Fxxxx` declarations and labels immediately before data blocks that were being referenced by pointer tables, such as `lbl_803F6788`, `lbl_803F6798`, `lbl_803F67D0`, `lbl_803F6818`, and `lbl_803F6888`.

Kept existing function pointer entries such as `func_802D73F0`, `func_8027C8B0`, and `func_802E8BCC` as symbolic function references while converting adjacent raw data-address entries to symbolic data labels.
The PR touched one file with a large mechanical diff: 1747 additions, 1353 deletions, and 256 hunks in `asm/data.s`.
smart_moves: Converted raw absolute addresses to labels instead of leaving opaque constants, which makes data pointer tables readable and enables grep-based cross-reference work.
Inserted global labels at the actual target data locations before changing pointer table references, avoiding dangling symbolic references.
Preserved address-derived label naming, minimizing semantic claims while still improving the assembly’s structure and linkability.
Handled repeated references consistently: repeated constants such as `0x803F67D0`, `0x803F6818`, `0x803F6888`, `0x803F6998`, and `0x803F6A28` were converted to the same corresponding label each time.
decomp_lessons: When a data table contains absolute addresses into the data segment, first create labels at the pointed-to data, then replace the numeric `.4byte` values with those labels.
Address-derived `lbl_XXXXXXXX` symbols are useful intermediate names when the structure or semantic name of the data is not yet known.
Large pointer-labeling passes can be valuable even without C decompilation because they improve future struct identification, table slicing, and cross-reference discovery.
For evidence quality, this PR is mostly diff-driven: there was no PR body, no issue comments, no review comments, and no reviews in the provided slice.
naming_conventions: Use `lbl_803Fxxxx` for data labels derived from data-segment addresses.
Use `.global lbl_803Fxxxx` immediately before a newly exposed data label when other table entries reference it.
Continue using `func_802xxxxx` names for function-pointer entries and `NULL` for null pointer slots.
Do not invent semantic names during broad mechanical labeling when only address-based evidence is available.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace `.4byte 0x803F8320`-style absolute data pointers with `.4byte lbl_803F8320`-style symbolic relocatable references.
Add labels at aligned or existing data boundaries without changing the data payload itself.
Use repeated raw address occurrences to confirm that multiple table entries point to the same target label.
Keep pointer table layout stable while only changing the representation of address operands.
review_feedback: No review feedback was present in the provided PR slice.
No PR body or human discussion was available; conclusions about intent come from the title, changed file list, and diff patterns.
follow_up_queries: Find remaining raw data-segment pointers with `grep -R "\.4byte 0x803F" asm/data.s`.
Search for unlabeled pointer-table targets near `lbl_803F3100` and `lbl_803F4D20`.
Audit newly global `lbl_803Fxxxx` blocks for potential semantic struct/table names once surrounding code is decompiled.
Look for repeated `lbl_803Fxxxx` references that may identify shared animation, jobj, or callback table data, but avoid renaming without stronger evidence.
